children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27–40, which are based on Reading Passage 3 below.

Self-propelled motion is a fundamental ability in many organisms. From the beating flagella of tiny plankton keeping afloat near the ocean surface to the playful perfection of dolphins surfing the bow wave of a ship, marine creatures have adopted a huge variety of styles, speeds, and methods of movement. Each species has its own particular need for the evolutionary developments that have taken place, but the basic requirements are the same—finding food, avoiding predation, seeking a mate or a safe place to have young, or migrating to an area with more favorable conditions.

The particular physical properties of water that most affect movement are density, viscosity (stickiness), and buoyancy. Seawater is about 800 times denser than air and nearly 100 times as viscous. Consequently, there is much more resistance to movement than on land, as anyone will know who has ever tried to wade through waist-deep water. However, with density comes much greater buoyancy, so that organisms need to spend relatively little energy to stay afloat. As they move through the ocean environment, organisms seek to make wave motion, currents, and natural turbulence work to their advantage, not to their detriment.

Most fishes have swim bladders to help them offset the density of their bodies and so maintain neutral buoyancy with minimal effort. These small, gas-filled chambers contain specialized networks of blood vessels that can add or remove gases such as oxygen and carbon dioxide. The ability to remain indefinitely at a constant depth without expending energy is especially important for slow-moving fishes that seek food in the shallows, or for those that hunt and scavenge in kelp forests. Active swimmers, such as mackerel, skipjacks, and sharks, do not have swim bladders because they need to change depth more rapidly than they could regulate the gas content. These fishes must swim forever or they will sink.

Many animals, especially the tiny zooplankton, have taken to a life of simply drifting near the surface, contentedly feeding on the microscopic phytoplankton and bacteria floating there. Although life among the plankton might seem easy, there is in fact a remarkable range of movement. Some tiny plankton only 1–2 mm in length actually travel long distances each day. Species that live below the level where sunlight reaches nightly swim hundreds of meters up to the surface to feed in the relative safety of darkness. At dawn, they sink back down in an effort to escape predators—a double journey equivalent to a person swimming 700 km a day.

It has taken marine creatures millions of years of evolution to overcome the chief deterrent to motion through a dense medium such as water—that of drag resistance. Swimming efficiency has been achieved by minimizing the three types of drag created by friction, turbulence, and body form. To reduce surface friction, the body must be smooth and rounded. In addition, the scales of most fishes are coated with slime to lubricate their passage through water. To reduce the turbulent drag created as water flows around the moving shape, a rounded front end and tapered back end are required. To reduce form drag, the cross-sectional area of the body should be minimal—a pencil shape would be ideal. The combined shape, taking into account all three types of drag, is the streamlined torpedo form of a tuna, the fastest-swimming of all fishes.

Speed is only one of three important aspects of swimming ability. Tuna, swordfish, and mackerel all specialize in fast, steady cruising, but there are many other fishes for whom sustained speed is less important, such as the barracuda. This formidable predator specializes in swift acceleration, and has a far higher success rate for its attacks than its steady-cruising cousins. The freshwater pike, which lurks in the shadows until its quarry is within striking distance and then lunges with great rapidity, achieves a remarkable 70–80 percent success rate. The third specialization is maneuverability, best demonstrated by the butterfly fishes. These have disk-shaped bodies that permit abrupt changes of track. Many fishes are generalists, being at least partly proficient in all three modes of movement.

Almost all fishes swim by undulation. Strong W-shaped muscles along the side of the body progressively contract and relax in sequence, from head to tail and from side to side, creating a traveling horizontal wave. The body is thrown into a series of curves that press sideways and back against the water, producing a forward thrust. The narrow, elongated forms of eels and sea snakes allow easy undulation along their full length. In contrast, the more stubby and inflexible bodies of armor-plated trunkfish use only the swish of their short tail fins to move themselves through the water. Most other fishes combine elements of both methods, coordinating powerful strokes of the tail fins with subtle body undulations.

A fish's fins also play a vital and versatile role. The vertically oriented dorsal and ventral fins on the back and belly control sideways motion, while up-and-down motion is controlled by the pectoral and pelvic fins on the fish's sides. Whereas the shape of the tail fin relates directly to speed—crescent-moon-shaped for fast cruising, broad and flat for acceleration—the style and arrangement of the other fins are crucial for maneuverability. Puffer fishes scull with tiny, oscillating pectoral fins, while butterfly fishes undulate their broad dorsal and ventral fins, twisting and turning with great precision through intricate coral reefs.
Which paragraph contains the following information?
, in boxes 27–31 on your answer sheet.
A strategy to avoid being attacked
How fish are able to keep afloat naturally
The physical process by which fish propel themselves ahead
A list of reasons why different creatures move from one place to another
How the medium of water both restricts and aids movement

explanationSection.text:
题目 27
答案：D
PDF 定位：一种避免被攻击的策略 D 段: "At dawn, they sink back down in an effort to escape predatorsa double journey.."
PDF 解析："拂晓时，它们又沉回深处，以線避捕食者 题干的 strategy to avoid being attacked = 这是一段相当于人类日游700公里的双 通过"夜晚上浮觅食，天亮下潜"来规避捕食 程旅途。" 者的攻击。其它段：A讲迁移/求偶等动因；B 讲水的物理特性；C讲缥；G/H讲推进与鱼 鳍。故选D.

题目 28
答案：C
PDF 定位：鱼如何能够自然地保持漂浮 C 段: "Most fishes have swim bladders.. maintain neutral buoyancy with minimal effort.
PDF 解析："keep afloat naturally" 对应 "maintain "大多数鱼拥有鱼螺，以极小代价维持中性 浮力。" neutralbuoyancy"。C段专述鱼如何调节气 体实现自然悬浮。

题目 29
答案：G
PDF 定位：鱼推进自身向前的物理过程 G 段: "..creating a traveling horizontal wave... producing a forward thrust.
PDF 解析："（肌肉》形成水平行进波..产生向前的推力。" 题干的physicalprocess即"体侧肌肉按序收 缩放松→体轴成波→对水产生反作用力→前 进"。其它段虽提到速度/形态，但唯G段详述 推进机制。

题目 30
答案：A
PDF 定位：不同生物从一处移动到另一处的各种原因清单 A 段: "..the basic requirements are the samefinding food, avoiding predation, seeking a mate... migrating to an area with more favorable conditions."
PDF 解析："基本需求相同觅食、避敌、求偶/育幼、迁 直接列举"为何而动"的清单式原因，完美对 移至条件更佳的区域。" 应题干。

题目 31
答案：B
PDF 定位：B 段: *..much more resistance to 水这种介质如何既限制又帮助运动 movement... However, with density comes much greater buoyancy.. organisms seek to make...turbulence work to their advantage..."
PDF 解析："restricts"=阻力/黏度；"aids"=浮力+借势 "水对运动的阻力更大..然面更高的密度带来 更大的浮力....生物试图让波浪/洋流/满流为 波流。B段兼述两面性。 已所用。"

children.text: from the passage for each answer.
Sudden attack on prey following period of lying in wait

explanationSection.text:
题目 32
答案：speed
PDF 定位：F : *Tuna, swordfish, and mackerel all 具备在长距离上保持同一速度的能力(剑鱼） specialize in fast, steady cruising.*
PDF 解析："金枪鱼、剑鱼、鱼都撞长快速而稳定的 行远距高遵航。故填speed。

题目 33
答案：acceleration
PDF 定位：快速加速(楼鱼） F : "the barracuda.. specializes in swift acceleration."
PDF 解析："与原文"swift acceleration* "楼鱼专长于迅速加速，" 题干*Rapid 同义替换。

题目 34
答案：freshwater pike / pike
PDF 定位：伏击后突然攻击猎物(潜伏一段时间) F FR: "The freshwater pike, which lurks. then lunges with great rapidity...*
PDF 解析："演水购鱼先潜伏。待疆物进入攻击距离便迅 题干摧述与句中"lurks→lunges"的伏击突 盈突装。" 装链条完全一致。允许写成pike（单词）或 freshwater pike (两调)。

题目 35
答案：butterfly fishes
PDF 定位：F : "The third specialization is 迅速改变方向 maneuverability, best demonstrated by the butterfly fishes... ermit abrupt changes of track.*
PDF 解析："第三种专长是机动性，蝶鱼最能体现，可突然 *rapid changes of direction" = "abrupt changes of track。故填物种 butterly 改变航向。" fishes,

attrs.alt: Diagram showing a fish's features for mobility, stability and combating drag resistance

explanationSection.text:
题目 36
答案：turbulent
PDF 定位：E段: "To reduce the turbulent drag.-. a 流线型、后部收窄以减少一阻力 rounded front end and tapered back end are required."
PDF 解析："要减少瑞流阻力，需要前端圆钝、后端渐缩。" "narrowing towards rear（后郎收窄）"对应 "tapered back end"，故为 turbulent (洲 流）阻力。

题目 37
答案：up-and-down
PDF 定位：H段: "up-and-down motion is controlled 控制上下运动的鳍 by the pectoral and pelvic fins..*
PDF 解析："上下运动由胸和腹鳍控制。" 图中对应侧面的胸/腹鳍。写法按题干连字符 up-anddown,

题目 38
答案：sideways
PDF 定位：控制横向/侧向运动的鳍 H 段: *The vertically oriented dorsal and ventral fins... control sideways motion."
PDF 解析："背鳍与腹（臀）鳍控制侧向运动。" 与37互补；图中指向背/腹（翌直取向）鳍。

题目 39
答案：maneuverability
PDF 定位：H 段: "the style and arrangement of the 对鱼类的机动性很重要的鳍 other fins are crucial for maneuverability."
PDF 解析：直接原词。美式拼写maneuverability；若用英 "其他鱼鳍的样式与组合对机动性至关重要。" 式manoeuvrability，按原文以美式为准。

题目 40
答案：form
PDF 定位：量小横截面积以减少一阻力 E 段: *To reduce form drag, the cross- sectional area of the body should be minimal.*
PDF 解析："要减少形状阻力，身体的横截面积应尽可 "cross-sectional body area minimal → 能小。" reduce form drag",
